1:
“Welcome everyone. Today, we’ll explore Tyk Streams — our native event-driven API framework inside the Tyk Gateway Enterprise Edition.
Tyk Streams unifies synchronous and asynchronous APIs under one policy, one control plane, and one analytics model.
By the end of this session, you’ll understand how to define, deploy, and govern streaming APIs through OpenAPI specifications using Tyk.”
3:
“When we talk about streaming APIs, we’re really talking about real-time, continuous data exchange — moving from the world of ‘request and response’ into ‘publish and subscribe.’ Instead of the client repeatedly asking for updates, the server proactively sends new data as soon as it’s available.”

[Server Push vs Client Pull] “In traditional REST APIs, communication is client-driven. The client has to pull data by making a request, getting a response, and then closing the connection. Streaming flips that model — it’s server push. Once a connection is established, the server pushes updates down the wire automatically.
Think of it like the difference between checking your inbox every few minutes versus getting instant notifications when a new message arrives.”

[Efficiency – No Repeated TLS Handshakes or Auth Overhead] “Because that connection stays open, we eliminate repetitive setup costs. In REST, each request typically involves:
A new TCP handshake,
A new TLS handshake,
And sometimes even a new authentication check.
With streaming, the handshake and authentication happen once — after that, the stream stays alive. This can save milliseconds per transaction and thousands of connection setups per minute in high-throughput systems.”

[Persistent Connection & Server State] “In a streaming API, the client keeps the connection open for as long as it’s needed — sometimes seconds, sometimes hours. The server maintains session state for that connection: who the client is, what they’re subscribed to, and what offset or topic they last received. This persistence is what allows for continuity — especially important in chat systems, live dashboards, and IoT telemetry feeds.”

[Common Patterns] “There are three common ways to achieve this persistent data flow:
Server-Sent Events (SSE): A simple, one-way stream from server to client over HTTP. Great for dashboards, monitoring, and notifications.
WebSockets: A full-duplex channel that supports messages in both directions. Perfect for real-time chat or gaming applications.
Webhooks: Not a live socket, but still event-driven — the server ‘calls back’ to a client endpoint whenever an event occurs. Webhooks are widely used for integrations — like GitHub notifying Jenkins, or Tyk Portal notifying external systems when a developer signs up.”

[Low Latency & Bidirectional Communication] “The big takeaway is latency. With traditional polling, you might have a few seconds of delay between an event happening and a client noticing. With streaming, that delay drops to milliseconds. The data arrives as soon as it’s produced. WebSockets even allow bidirectional communication, so clients can send control signals — for example, ‘pause this feed’ or ‘filter to topic X’ — in real time.”

[Foundation for Event-Driven Architectures] “This capability forms the foundation for event-driven architecture — where services react to events instead of waiting to be queried. It’s the underlying principle behind systems like Kafka, MQTT, or any reactive microservice design. Tyk Streams builds directly on this concept — it allows you to handle event-driven, real-time APIs natively in the same Gateway that handles REST and GraphQL.”
4:
“On this slide, I want to show you visually the fundamental shift between how traditional REST APIs communicate and how streaming APIs work.
On the left, we have the classic request–response model that we’ve all worked with. On the right, we have a persistent, event-driven model — the foundation of streaming APIs.”
“Let’s start with the traditional model. Here, communication is entirely client-driven. The client initiates every interaction by sending an HTTP request — typically GET, POST, PUT, or DELETE.
The server processes that request, returns a single complete response, and then — that’s it — the connection closes.
This model is stateless, which means the server doesn’t remember anything about that client once the request is done. Every new request has to include everything the server needs to know — authentication, query parameters, headers — all of it, every time.
It’s also on-demand — data is retrieved or modified only when the client explicitly asks. This makes REST simple and scalable for many workloads, but it’s inefficient for scenarios that need continuous updates.”
“Think about a stock price feed or a live chat app — with REST, the client has to keep asking for updates, often every few seconds. That’s a lot of unnecessary traffic.”
“Now let’s look at the streaming side. In this model, the client still initiates the connection — but instead of closing it after a single response, it establishes a persistent connection using a protocol like WebSockets or Server-Sent Events (SSE).
Once connected, the client can subscribe to specific topics or data streams — for example, a chat channel, a sensor feed, or a stock ticker.
From there, the communication becomes push-based. The server proactively sends data to the client whenever an event occurs — no more constant polling. This is the same principle that powers live dashboards, multiplayer games, IoT telemetry, and financial trading systems.”
Stateful Connection
“Unlike REST, streaming APIs are often stateful. The server keeps track of connected clients — who they are, what topics they’re subscribed to, and where they left off in the stream. This persistent context enables richer real-time interactions, but it also means you have to manage connection lifecycles, reconnections, and resource cleanup carefully.”
“So, if we put the two side-by-side:
Both have their place. REST is perfect for CRUD-style operations and one-off queries, while streaming APIs shine when immediacy and responsiveness are critical.”
“So the key takeaway here is this: REST waits for you to ask. Streaming just keeps talking — in real time.
If your system needs instant updates or event-driven workflows, streaming APIs are your friend. And the beauty of Tyk Streams is that it lets you manage both REST and streaming endpoints within the same Gateway, using the same policies, security, and analytics stack.”
5:
Event-driven architecture (EDA) is a software design pattern where components communicate asynchronously by producing and reacting to events, which are changes in state.
“Now that we understand what streaming APIs are, let’s zoom out and see where they fit — in an Event-Driven Architecture, or EDA.
Here’s the key idea: producers emit events, consumers react to them. A producer doesn’t know or care who’s listening — it just broadcasts an event saying, ‘Something happened.’
For example, an Order Service emits an order.created event. That message is sent to an event broker like Kafka or RabbitMQ. From there, any number of consumers — say, Payment, Shipping, or Analytics services — can pick it up and act on it.
This pattern makes the entire system asynchronous and decoupled. Producers and consumers evolve independently. If a consumer is down or slow, the broker queues the event until it’s ready — no service-to-service blocking, no retry storms.”

(Point to the broker in the diagram) “The event broker is the backbone of this architecture. It provides message routing, buffering, and replay. Kafka is a great example — it can store days or weeks of events, letting you replay them if you need to rebuild a dataset. MQTT, on the other hand, is extremely lightweight, perfect for IoT sensors that send small messages over flaky connections. AMQP is common in enterprise systems where guaranteed delivery and routing patterns matter most.”

(Pause and summarize visually) “So instead of chaining synchronous HTTP calls, we’ve introduced an intermediary — the broker — that decouples timing, scale, and availability. This is how we get scalability and resilience.
If one consumer fails, others still process their events. If traffic spikes, producers keep publishing and the broker absorbs the burst. That’s why most modern cloud-native systems — from e-commerce to IoT — rely on event-driven patterns.”

(Transition to Tyk Streams) “Tyk Streams fits directly into this model. It lets your Gateway act as both a producer (publishing HTTP events into Kafka or MQTT) and a consumer (streaming messages out to WebSockets or SSE). So you can bridge the gap between traditional APIs and event-driven systems seamlessly — same Gateway, same governance, real-time delivery.”
6:
“Now that we’ve covered the fundamentals of streaming APIs, let’s look at how Tyk as a platform supports them — from simple push updates to full event-driven architectures.
Tyk’s architecture supports advanced communication protocols that let you manage SSE, WebSockets, and event brokers with the same governance, security, and analytics you already use for REST and GraphQL APIs.”

🟦 Server-Sent Events (SSE)
“First, Server-Sent Events (SSE). Tyk provides native SSE proxying out of the box. This means Tyk can sit between your backend event stream and your frontend clients, proxying real-time server-push data over long-lived HTTP connections.
The beauty of this approach is that SSE doesn’t require a new protocol — it uses HTTP, so it’s lightweight, firewall-friendly, and works seamlessly in browsers through the EventSource API.
Tyk applies all the usual API management controls here — authentication, rate limiting, quota enforcement, and analytics. It’s ideal for real-time dashboards, notifications, and status updates where the client only needs one-way communication from the server.”

🟩 WebSockets
“Next, WebSockets — Tyk offers comprehensive WebSocket proxying. We can maintain persistent, bidirectional connections between clients and backend services.
This is perfect for two-way communication use cases: chat systems, collaborative tools, or live dashboards where both client and server continuously exchange data.
Tyk can also apply the same security and policy layers — so a WebSocket connection can still require a JWT, can still be rate-limited by message frequency, and will still appear in your analytics and quota reports.”

🧩 Tyk Streams for Event Brokers
“Finally, Tyk Streams — this is the extension of the Gateway that brings broker-level event-driven integration into the picture.
With Tyk Streams, we can connect your APIs directly to event brokers like Kafka, MQTT, or any AMQP 0.9.1-compatible system. This lets you blend asynchronous event processing with traditional APIs, all under one control plane.
Tyk Streams supports protocol mediation — so you can:
Expose Kafka topics as WebSocket APIs, making internal event streams available to web clients.
Convert MQTT messages into HTTP webhooks, bridging IoT systems to cloud apps.
Or even bridge between brokers — for example, Kafka → MQTT or AMQP → Kafka.
This allows you to unify your messaging layer with your API layer — bringing security, observability, and rate control into the event space.”

🧠 Wrap-Up Summary
“So in summary: Tyk supports SSE and WebSockets natively for real-time client communication, and extends into brokers like Kafka, MQTT, AMQP through Tyk Streams, providing full protocol mediation between REST, WebSocket, and event-driven systems.
All of this runs inside the same Gateway runtime, with consistent policy enforcement, security, analytics, and developer portal integration. In other words, Tyk lets you manage every communication model — request/response, event-driven, or streaming — under one platform.”

8:
“Now that we know what Tyk supports, let’s talk about what truly sets it apart — the unified lifecycle. Whether your API is synchronous (like REST or GraphQL) or asynchronous (like Kafka or WebSocket), you don’t need separate platforms or brokers — Tyk Streams brings them all together under one Gateway and one policy model.”

🔹 Same Tyk Security, Rate Limits, and Analytics
“One of Tyk’s greatest strengths is consistency. Tyk Streams is built directly into the Gateway middleware chain, so streaming APIs inherit all the same capabilities as your REST APIs.
That means:
Authentication via API keys, JWTs, OAuth 2.0, or mTLS.
Rate limiting and quotas, applied per connection or per message.
Policies and RBAC still govern access to topics and streams.
Analytics are recorded the same way — every message or event can appear in Tyk Pump exporters and dashboards.
You get a single pane of glass for both REST and streaming traffic — no need to stitch together multiple observability stacks.”

🔹 Connect REST ↔ Kafka ↔ WebSocket Seamlessly
“This is where Tyk Streams shines — protocol mediation. You can connect:
REST producers that publish events via HTTP POST,
to Kafka or AMQP brokers that handle reliable distribution,
and then stream those events to clients via WebSocket or SSE.
The Gateway acts as a translator:
HTTP on one side,
Kafka or MQTT in the middle,
WebSocket or SSE on the other.
No extra code, no custom service — it’s all declared in your API definition using x-tyk-streaming.”
(Gesture to diagram or draw it)
[REST Client] → HTTP POST → Tyk Gateway → Kafka Topic → Tyk Gateway → WebSocket/SSE → [Live Clients]

“This closes the loop between synchronous APIs and event-driven systems — all with the same Gateway process.”

🔹 No External Broker Configuration in Gateway
“Unlike traditional event gateways or message routers, Tyk doesn’t require you to embed or configure a broker inside the Gateway. Instead, Tyk connects directly to your external brokers — Kafka, MQTT, AMQP— using their native protocols.
That means:
No extra Kafka Connectors or Message Bus add-ons.
No duplicate configuration.
You can use your existing enterprise broker infrastructure, while Tyk handles the API edge, security, and mediation.”

🔹 Unified Lifecycle Management
“Everything is managed as part of the API lifecycle, not as a separate integration. You can:
Version your event streams just like REST APIs.
Apply policies, track analytics, and publish them to the Developer Portal.
Automate deployment and configuration through CI/CD or Tyk Sync.
Govern streaming APIs alongside REST and GraphQL in the same Dashboard.
So from design → deploy → monitor → monetize, both synchronous and asynchronous APIs follow the same flow — one lifecycle, one governance model, one Gateway.”

🧠 Wrap-Up Summary
“In short, Tyk Streams doesn’t just add new protocols — it extends the entire API management lifecycle to real-time and event-driven APIs.
You get:
The same security and rate-limit policies,
Seamless REST ↔ Kafka ↔ WebSocket connectivity,
No broker bloat or duplication,
And a unified way to design, deploy, and manage every kind of API.”
9:
1. Powered by BENTO Stream Processor
“Everything inside Tyk Streams is powered by Bento, our internal streaming engine. This processor is capable of transforming, filtering, enriching, and routing events in flight.
You can do simple mappings with Bloblang, or you can do more advanced operations like branching a message to two different outputs, running HTTP lookups for enrichment, adding timestamps, cleaning up fields, or implementing masking.
Bento is memory-buffered and optimized for high throughput. It lets us give you a flexible and programmable streaming pipeline without requiring additional microservices or ETL frameworks.”

2. Defined in x-tyk-streaming (OAS Only)
“All Tyk Streams functionality is declared using the OpenAPI extension x-tyk-streaming. This gives you a clean, versionable, GitOps-friendly way to declare your streaming flows alongside your REST API definition.
Because it’s part of the OAS model, the same workflows apply: linting, validation, Dashboard management, Tyk Sync automation, and version control. Only OAS APIs support Streams — Tyk Classic definitions do not support this feature.”
10:
3. Inputs → Processors → Outputs
“This is the core model. Every stream starts with an input — HTTP POST, WebSocket, Kafka, MQTT, AMQP, etc. Then comes the processor stage, where Bento applies transformations or enrichments. Finally, we have outputs — which can be Kafka topics, WebSockets, SSE endpoints, or webhooks.
This pipeline allows you to build very powerful flows: REST → Kafka Kafka → WebSocket MQTT → Webhook Kafka → WebSocket + Webhook (fan-out)
All inside the same Gateway, with full governance.”
11:
“This slide highlights why Tyk Streams is more than just a streaming add-on — it is a protocol mediation engine integrated directly into the API Gateway. Tyk doesn't just proxy messages; it translates across protocols while keeping all the security, governance, and analytics you already rely on.”

🔹 Protocol Mediation: HTTP ↔ Kafka, SSE, MQTT, AMQP

“Tyk Streams can sit in the middle of different communication worlds — REST, WebSockets, SSE, and event brokers like Kafka or MQTT.
For example:

A client can POST JSON to Tyk → Tyk publishes it to Kafka.

Kafka emits another event → Tyk streams it to WebSocket clients.

MQTT devices publish telemetry → Tyk converts it into HTTP webhooks for your cloud services.

This eliminates the need for custom integration microservices or broker-specific gateways. You define the flow once in your API definition.”

🔹 Security via Existing Middleware Chain

“All streaming endpoints reuse the same middleware chain used for REST:

Authentication (JWT, OAuth, mTLS, API Keys)

Rate limits and quotas

Policies with access rights

RBAC and tenancy separation

This means your WebSocket /ws endpoint or your SSE /sse endpoint is just as secure and governed as /users or /orders.
There’s no bypass, no separate security model — everything flows through the same Gateway pipeline.”

🔹 Transformations with Bloblang/Bento

“Bento is the processor engine behind Streams.
Using Bloblang, you can:

Reshape payloads

Normalize schema

Add timestamps or metadata

Filter messages before publishing

Perform HTTP enrichment to external services

This lets you build smart pipelines — e.g., adding device coordinates, cleaning malformed fields, or creating multiple downstream streams from one input.”
12:
🔹 Developer Portal via portal_webhook
“One of the sleeper features is the ability to integrate Streams with the Developer Portal. Using the portal_webhook output:
You can monetize event streams
Trigger webhooks based on developer subscriptions
Securely retrieve webhook credentials and deliver messages
Notify external partners or SaaS systems
This makes event-driven APIs first-class citizens in your API product strategy.”

🔹 Analytics via Pump Exporters
“You get full observability with zero extra setup. Streams reuse the Gateway’s analytics recorder, so every connection, message, error, and timing metric goes into the Pump pipeline.
From there, you can export to:
Postgres or Mongo (Dashboard analytics)
Prometheus (metrics)
Datadog (APM)
Elastic (log analysis)
And you can shard analytics per API or per organization for multi-tenant environments.”

🧠 Wrap-Up Summary
“In short, Tyk Streams turns the Gateway into a unified protocol mediator. It handles REST, WebSockets, SSE, Kafka, MQTT, AMQP — all under one control plane, with the same auth, policies, analytics, and governance you already trust.
This is what enables true API/EDA convergence inside Tyk.”
13:
“Now let’s look under the hood at how Tyk Streams processes events as they come into and out of the Gateway. The internal flow follows four main components: Middleware → Manager → Instance → Analytics.”

🔹 1. Middleware
“The Middleware is the first piece of the puzzle. Every streaming request — whether it’s a POST to /chat, or a GET to /sse, or a WebSocket upgrade request to /ws — is intercepted by the Tyk middleware chain.
At this layer:
Authentication is verified
Rate limits are enforced
Quotas, RBAC, and policies apply
The request path is matched to a registered stream
If no stream is registered for that path, Tyk returns a 404. If it matches, the Middleware hands the request to the Stream Manager.”

🔹 2. Stream Manager
“The Stream Manager is the orchestrator for the streaming layer. It does many things behind the scenes:
It looks at the API’s x-tyk-streaming block and builds a configuration hash.
If a matching stream instance already exists, it reuses it — no need to rebuild Bento pipelines.
If not, it creates a new instance and caches it.
It performs garbage collection — removing inactive stream instances and closing idle connections.
It also keeps track of active subscribers, which is especially important for SSE and WebSocket outputs.
Think of it as the lifecycle manager for your event pipelines.”

🔹 3. Instance
“The Stream Instance is where the actual data movement happens. Each instance contains the Bento pipeline you defined: Inputs → Processors → Outputs.
It is responsible for:
Connecting to Kafka/MQTT/AMQP as needed
Accepting HTTP POST or WebSocket messages
Applying transformations (Bloblang, HTTP enrichment)
Streaming messages out over WebSocket or SSE
Handling backpressure, fan-out, or batching as configured
A single instance can serve many concurrent clients depending on the output type.”

🔹 4. Analytics
“Tyk Streams uses the existing analytics recorder already embedded in the Gateway. Each message that flows through a stream instance generates metrics such as:
Message count
Processing success/failure
Connection duration
Disconnection reasons
Latency (if applicable)
These analytics are then shipped to your backend using Tyk Pump, which allows you to visualize, alert, and monitor streaming APIs just like REST APIs.”

🔹 Stream Path Registration
“When a stream is defined, Tyk automatically registers default paths based on the connectors you use.
For example:
/post is mapped to HTTP ingestion (http_server)
/post/ws maps to WebSocket ingestion (websocket_server)
/get/stream is used for SSE or WebSocket output
These paths abstract away the complexity — developers only interact with simple, predictable endpoints.”

🧠 Wrap-Up Summary
“Together, the Middleware, Manager, Instance, and Analytics components form the processing backbone of Tyk Streams. This architecture ensures that every streaming endpoint benefits from the same security, governance, and observability as your REST and GraphQL APIs, all while enabling scalable event-driven flows.”
14:
“Let me walk you through what actually happens inside Tyk when a request enters the Stream API, because this is where the power of the platform becomes very visible.
We start at the top:
1. The client sends an HTTP POST or GET
This could be your producer system, a microservice, a mobile app — any system capable of making an HTTP call. Tyk Stream treats the incoming call as a first-class API request. That means you instantly gain API-level governance: authentication, quotas, rate limits, schema validation, mTLS if needed — all before the event even touches your streaming layer.

2. Middleware validates and authenticates
This is the same mature middleware chain used by our Gateway:
API Keys
JWT
HMAC
Client certificates
Request size limits
JSON schema validation
This step ensures you’re not pushing invalid or unauthorised data into Kafka, which is a major advantage over raw ingestion services like Kinesis Stream.

3. Routed to the Bento handler
Once the request passes validation, it enters the Bento engine, which is the internal streaming processor inside Tyk. Think of Bento as a programmable pipeline: it takes your incoming request and maps it to the configured stream workflow.
This is where Tyk loads:
your stream definition
the processors you’ve configured
and the input/output plug-ins that drive the routing.

4. Processed through input/output connectors
Inside Bento, the message flows through your stream pipeline:
It enters through the HTTP Input
It passes through any optional processors (e.g., JSON mutation, metadata injection, filtering, enrichment, batching)
Then Tyk sends it to the configured output connector, such as Kafka.
This stage is where Tyk Streams differentiates itself: you can fan-out to multiple topics, route based on message content, or apply transformations — all declarative, no code.

5. Analytics logged
Finally, once the event is dispatched, Tyk logs analytics exactly like any other API call. You get:
per-tenant usage metrics
per-key request visibility
OTel-compatible tracing
full observability of your ingestion layer
This is something you don’t get easily with traditional streaming systems.

So the lifecycle here is: secure → validate → process → dispatch → observe. And because this sits on the Gateway, you get the full APIM governance stack on top of your streaming layer — which is the core advantage of using Tyk Streams over raw ingestion services.”
16: “Let’s walk through a simple end-to-end example using Tyk Streams. A user types ‘Tell me a joke’ in the browser, Tyk publishes that into Kafka, a backend processes it, and Tyk delivers the response back to the browser. This demonstrates Tyk as both a producer and consumer gateway for async flows.”
17: “Here, Tyk acts as the producer. The browser sends a normal HTTP POST request to /chat. Tyk Streams converts that into a Kafka message and places it onto a topic. The backend Joker Service consumes it. The client doesn't need to know Kafka exists — it only talks HTTP to Tyk.”
18: “Next, Tyk becomes a consumer. When the Joker Service posts the joke to another Kafka topic, Tyk Streams listens to it and pushes the joke out to the client. This can be done using SSE or WebSockets depending on what the client supports.”
19: “For the demo we’ll run Tyk and Kafka using Docker, clone the demo repo, and use curl for testing. We also need a Dashboard license because Streams is configured through the Dashboard.”
20:
“This repository contains a docker-compose setup that includes Tyk Gateway, Dashboard, Redis, and Kafka. Cloning it is our starting point.”
“Tyk Streams is off by default. We enable it by setting these environment variables. Note that we must use the Enterprise Gateway image because Streams is an enterprise feature."
21: “Running ./up.sh spins up the entire environment. At the end it prints out Dashboard credentials that we’ll use shortly.”
22: “This API definition represents the /chat endpoint. When we POST to it, Tyk publishes messages into Kafka. We load it into Tyk using a simple curl command.”
23: “Next we create the consumer API, which subscribes to a Kafka topic and exposes an SSE endpoint to deliver messages back to clients.”
24: “The Joker Service is a small script that listens to the chat topic and replies with jokes to the discussion topic. This simulates any backend microservice.”
25:
“In one terminal we connect to the SSE endpoint. This gives us a real-time event stream from Tyk. Any message Kafka emits will show up here.”
“Now in a second terminal we POST messages to the producer API. Tyk forwards them to Kafka, the Joker Service responds, and the SSE stream prints the joke instantly.”
26: “So in just a few steps, we showed how Tyk Streams can bridge HTTP and Kafka in both directions. The browser never touches Kafka, the backend never touches HTTP or WebSockets, and Tyk sits in the middle providing policy, security, and real-time delivery.”
